coredata.dc:title: Recyclable, robust and shape memory vitrified polyisoprene composite prepared through a green methodology
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Zhen
Wu, Ruiyao
Yang, Yinxin
Huang, Lingyun
Fan, Weifeng
Dai, Quanquan
Cui, Long
He, Jianyun
Bai, Chenxi
coredata.dc:description: Due to the weak mechanical performance of raw rubber, it cannot meet the actual use requirements, thus cross-linking and reinforcement have been the eternal theme of rubber materials. However, this permanent crosslinking structure brings the dilemma that the rubber materials cannot be recycled after the life cycle, which causes energy waste and serious environmental pollution. Herein, we present a facile and green methodology to prepare covalently cross-linked yet recyclable and robust polyisoprene-based vitrimer. The carboxylated carbon nanodots (CNDs) were selected as both cross-linkers and reinforcing components to react with epoxidized polyisoprene (EPI) for the construction of dynamic cross-linked networks with exchangeable β-hydroxyl ester bonds for the first time. The dynamic covalent linkages endowed the EPI-based vitrimers with reprocessability and excellent shape memory performance. In addition, the dynamic exchangeable interface enables CNDs well-dispersed in the EPI matrix, which exhibited high reinforcement efficiency on the mechanical properties of EPI composite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-hydroxyl ester bonds
Recyclable
Shape memory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the interface cross-linking network
Reshape and reprocessability of the vitrified rubber composite

Y9390002 ), and the Special Fund Supported Project for High-tech Industrialization of Science and Technology Cooperation between Jilin Province and the Chinese Academy of Sciences ( 2019SYHZ0003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of CNDs, EPI, Uncured-CNDs-7.5 and Cured-CNDs-7.5 (B) Bound rubber, cross-linking density and swelling ratio of CNDs samples. (C) Typical stress-strain curves of CNDs samples. (D) Temperature dependence of the storage modulus and tan δ of CNDs samples.

(A) and (B) Creep test of sample CNDs-5.0, CNDs-7.5, CNDs-10.0, CNDs-12.5 at 180 °C and 30 °C, respectively. (C) Stress relaxation of CNDs-7.5 at different temperature (D) Fitting of τ* of the Arrhenius equation for CNDs-7.5.

(A) Rectangular splines can be reshaped into various shapes (zigzag, heart-shaped, Spiral rolls et al.) (B) Reprocess of CNDs-7.5 through hot press. (C) Typical stress−strain curves of CNDs-7.5 after reprocessing. (D) Recovery ratio of mechanical properties of all samples after reprocessing.
(A) Images of qualitative study demonstrating shape memory behavior. (B) Quantitative shape memory and shape reconfiguration cycles for the vitrified rubber composite.
Schematic diagram of constructing a dynamic reinforced network.
Summary table of CNDs samples (Including sol fraction, swelling ratio, cross-linking density, bound rubber content, elongation at break, tensile strength).

row.entry: Molar ratio of epoxy group/carboxyl group

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (The error in the table is standard deviation,3 specimens for each sample).

head.ce:title: Recyclable, robust and shape memory vitrified polyisoprene composite prepared through a green methodology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High-Performance Synthetic Rubber and Its Composite Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the weak mechanical performance of raw rubber, it cannot meet the actual use requirements, thus cross-linking and reinforcement have been the eternal theme of rubber materials. However, this permanent crosslinking structure brings the dilemma that the rubber materials cannot be recycled after the life cycle, which causes energy waste and serious environmental pollution. Herein, we present a facile and green methodology to prepare covalently cross-linked yet recyclable and robust polyisoprene-based vitrimer. The carboxylated carbon nanodots (CNDs) were selected as both cross-linkers and reinforcing components to react with epoxidized polyisoprene (EPI) for the construction of dynamic cross-linked networks with exchangeable β-hydroxyl ester bonds for the first time. The dynamic covalent linkages endowed the EPI-based vitrimers with reprocessability and excellent shape memory performance. In addition, the dynamic exchangeable interface enables CNDs well-dispersed in the EPI matrix, which exhibited high reinforcement efficiency on the mechanical properties of EPI composite.

ce:section.ce:para: Rubber is one of the most widely used polymer materials [1], which can be seen from our daily necessities to various machine parts that support the operation of modern industrial society. Whereas, in fact most types of the un-crosslinked rubber molecules (raw rubber or called gum) are non-polar linear structure, with weak intermolecular forces and low cohesive energy density (most are lower than 300 MJ/m3), this also account of why the glass-transition temperature (T g) of rubber materials is far below room temperature [2]. It can be said that none of raw rubber can meet the requirements in the actual application fields. Thence, crosslinking and reinforcement have been the eternal theme of rubber materials science [3,4]. In practice, the general method of reinforcement in the field of rubber materials is to mix fillers, especially nano-fillers, which is considered to be a necessary condition for effective rubber reinforcement. Although carbon black (CB) and silica currently used on a large scale also belong to the category of nano-fillers, however, they generally show weak interface interaction with the rubber matrix, and often require a high fraction loading, which fails to realize the high-efficient reinforcement of nano-fillers [5,6]. Moreover, despite traditional cross-linking and reinforcement strategy endows the rubber material high mechanical properties, it cannot be reprocessed or reshaped for the reason that the cross-linked structure is a permanent one, and it is also difficult to be degraded in the natural environment, which caused serious environmental problems at the end of their service life [7–9]. In order to alleviate the current dilemma, the development of cross-linking and reinforcement strategies for the fabrication of brand-new rubber materials with recoverability and reprocessability are not only a pressing need but also has become a new scientific theme in the field of rubber materials [10,11].

Covalent adaptable networks (CANs) [12], is new class of polymer materials contain dynamic covalent chemistry (DCC) that can “adapt” their network architecture in response to an external trigger or stimulus (heat, light ph et al.), in spite of their cross-linked nature. In 2011, Leibler and coworkers [13] innovatively proposed the concept of vitrimer, which featured as thermally activated associative CANs that show Arrhenius-like viscosity dependence like those of vitreous silica. They implant β-hydroxyl ester into epoxy networks that can rearrange the network topology by transesterification reactions without depolymerization and showed insoluble and recyclable properties, which exhibits broad application prospects in the field of polymer crosslinking strategy. Since then, some new dynamic exchange bonds have been exploited and applied to polymer crosslinking networks. For instance, in 2015, Winne, Du Prez and coworkers [14] introduced a catalyst-free transamination of vinylogous urethanes into the polymer network, achieving four cycles of recovery by consecutive grinding/compression process without significant mechanical or chemical destruction. In 2017, Zhibin Guan et al. [15] embedded siloxanes as cross-linkers for hydroxy-functionalized polystyrene backbones. Notably, they found that the inclusion of basic amine moieties in the cross-linkers can act as an internal catalyst to facilitate the exchange of silane. Also in 2017, Nicolaÿ and Leibler [16] grafted dioxaborolanes into polystyrene, poly(methyl methacrylate), and high-density polyethylene, endowing them with superior dimensional stability, chemical resistance and multiple times processing ability by extrusion or injection molding. As aforementioned, cross-linking is also an indispensable step for the practical application of rubber materials, therefore, if the concept of CANs strategy can be introduced into the rubber network and realize the reprocessing and recycling of rubber materials, it will greatly alleviate the current dilemma that a large amount of waste rubber being discarded after end-of-life usage.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs), as emergent fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial, have attracted considerable attention since first reported in 2004 [17]. On account of their low toxicity, photoluminescence, good biocompatibility and facile preparation [18–20], a great deal of different kinds of CNDs (functionalized with carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, etc) are synthesized and applied in various fields, including biological (cell labeling, bioimaging) [21], catalysis (photocatalysis, acid-base catalysis) [22], photovoltaic devices (supercapacitors), etc [23]. However, there are relatively few researches on its application as a new type of self-functionalized nanomaterial in the field of rubber composite. For instance, Guo et al. [24] used high-functionality CNDs to construct multiple sacrificial sites in the diene-rubber network, achieving an increase both in strength and toughness. Wu et al. [25] fabricated a mechanically robust rubber composite by employing nitrogen doped CNDs with chloroprene rubber. Yue et al. [26] adopt solvothermal treatment to prepare silane-functionalized CNDs for the first time as novel compatibilizers of the methyl vinyl silicone/polyurethane thermoplastic rubber blends. Nevertheless, the scanty fabrication of the CNDs-based rubber materials mostly requires the use of organic solvents to date, which are toxic, expensive and cumbersome in post-processing. Besides, few of them [45] can achieve reprocessing and recycling at the end of their service life. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore a green synthetic methodology for recyclable CNDs-based rubber materials.

In order to achieve the balance of crosslinking, reinforcement and recycling, in this work, we adopt a green methodology with a solvent-free process to construct dynamic β-hydroxyl ester bonds at the interface between epoxidized polyisoprene rubber (EPI) and carboxyl functionalized CNDs, in one step achieving cross-linking, reinforcement and reprocessability of vitrified polyisoprene composite as illustrated in Scheme 1 . First, according to the Dhamodharan's work [27], we made some modifications and synthesized carboxylated functionalized CNDs by one-step solvation-free method, which has high yield, and simple post-processing, and can be prepared on a large scale with high practicability. Second, the functionalized CNDs and EPI was uniformly mixed in the internal mixer and then hot pressed to obtain this vitrified rubber composite material. The epoxy group in EPI and the carboxyl group on the surface of CNDs can form dynamic β-hydroxyl ester bonds, which can relax the network stress via continuous transesterification reaction at elevated temperature and catalytic conditions, endowing the samples with the ability to be reshaped and recyclable. Moreover, these rubber composite materials can also be used as shape memory materials.

Epoxidized polyisoprene (EPI) was supplied by Key Laboratory of High-Performance Synthetic Rubber and its Composite Materials (HSRCM), Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, with molecular weight Mn ~420 kDa and epoxidation degree of 43% (Fig. S6 and Fig. S7.); Imidazole (≥98%) was purchased from BEIJING SHIJI, Maleic Anhydride (≥99.5%) was received from XiLONG SCIENTIFIC; Anhydrous zinc acetate (Zn(Ac)2, ≥99.5%), 1,2-Dimethylimidazole (DMI, ≥98%) and Sebacic acid (SA, ≥98%) were purchased from Energy Chemical, China. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

The synthesis of functionalized carbon nanodots (CNDs) refers to the literature [27] with some modification. Firstly, the imidazole and maleic anhydride are mixed in a constant temperature agate mill at a molar ratio of 1:2, and then the reaction is triggered by a vigorously mill. After the reaction, the product was quickly transferred to the reagent bottle, purifying with a Soxhlet extractor, and then placed in a vacuum oven for drying. The carboxylic acid content of the nanodots measured by acid-base titration is 2.6 mmol/g. The UV spectrum of carbon nanodots exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at 206 nm (Fig. S1.), which corresponds to the skeletal electronic excitation arising out of an n-π* transition of the surface carboxylate groups. TEM test results show that the average particle size of carbon nano-dots is about 20–30 nm (Fig. S4.). X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CNDs have a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 210 (Fig. S3.), which indicate that it is an amorphous structure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the presence of C, N, and O elements. Moreover, C 1S and O 1S expanded spectrum further confirmed the presence of C and O in the form of CO (285.7eV), C–OH (532.8eV), which indicate that functionalized CNDs with surface carboxyl groups have been successfully synthesized (Fig. S5.).

Firstly, Stoichiometric amount of EPI, zinc acetate, and 1,2-dimethylimidazole are put in an internal mixer for mixing, and then a two-roll mill is used to produce flakes. After that, the EPI samples are hot-pressed at 180 °C according to the optimum time tested by the vulcameter. Functionalized nano-carbon dots are 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5 per 100 g of EPI respectively. The parts of 1,2-dimethylimidazole and zinc acetate relative to the carboxyl group are 35 and 15 mol%, respectively. 1,2-Dimethylimidazole acts as an accelerator to promote crosslinking kinetics [28]. Zinc acetate can not only promote the esterification of epoxy/acid, but also promote the transesterification between hydroxyl and ester groups [29–31]. The vitrified rubber composites are represented as CNDs-x, where x represented x phr CNDs.
For comparison, sebacic acid with the same carboxyl group content as 12.5 parts of (CNDs) is used as a cross-linking molecule, and the operation process is the same as mentioned above.

1H NMR spectra were obtained using an AVANCE 400 MHz spectrometer at 25 °C (solvent: CDCl3). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a BRUKE Vertex-70 FTIR spectrometer (KBr pellet technique) from 4000 to 500 cm−1. Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was performed on a TA instrument Q850DMA at a heating rate of 3 °C/min from −70 to 100 °C with a frequency of 1 Hz. The sample sheet was cut into rectangle with dimension of 15
×
4
×
1 mm. For the stress relaxation experiment, the sample was equilibrated at a specific test temperature for 5 min and a 5% strain is applied. For creep experiments, a nominal stress of 0.2 MPa at room temperature and 0.15 MPa at 180 °C was applied on the sample after a 10 min temperature equilibration, respectively. Vulcanization experiments were performed at Scientific Laboratory Hydraulic Press Type LP-S-50. Compound flat is conducted at Scientific Laboratory Rubber Two-Roll Mill Type LRMR-S-150/Oil with Oil Heating and Cooling System and Hydraulic QUIC Open. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded using a Cary 50 (Instrument Version 3.00) UV–vis spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 with Mgka radiation (1253.6 eV). The tensile strength was conducted on an Instron 5689 universal testing machine and the dumbbell-shaped samples with dimension of 60 mm × 4 mm × 1 mm and a gauge length of 25 mm, the drawing rate was 500 mm/min. Three specimens were measured for each sample, and the average value and standard deviation were calculated. Equilibrium swelling experiments were conducted by immersing cured samples in toluene at 30 °C for 72h. The cross-linking density was calculated based on the classical Flory−Rehner equation. The gel fraction is defined as GF
=
M 3
M 1
and the swelling ratio is calculated by
M 2
−
M 3
M 3
, where M
1 is the weight of the sample before swelling, M
2 and M
3 are the weight when the sample reaches swelling equilibrium and the weight after vacuum drying. Detailed information can be found in the support information. Torque Rheometer (Shanghai KeChuang Rubber Plastics Machinery Set LTd, XSS-300). Gel permeation chromatograph (Waters 515 GPC, Waters Corp.) Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) perform on a Bruker-AXS X-ray diffractometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were conducted on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN to clarify the CNDs morphology. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) patterns were acquired by using XL30 ESEMFEG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the interface cross-linking network
ce:section.ce:para: For the interface cross-linking reaction between EPI and CNDs, FT-IR spectroscopy is an effective means to clarify the reaction between the carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs and the oxirane groups in EPI. In the FT-IR spectrum of CNDs (Fig. 1 A.), the absorption peak at 1710 cm−1 corresponding to the ν(–COO–), arises out of the carboxylate groups on the surface of the CNDs, and the absorption peaks at 3150 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of = CH and CC, respectively [27]. XPS (Fig. S5.) results C 1S and O 1S expanded spectrum further confirmed the presence of C and O in the form of CO(285.7eV), C–OH (532.8eV) on the surface of CNDs. The above results indicate that we successful synthesis of carboxyl functionalized CNDs. Compared with EPI and Uncured-CNDs-7.5, the absorption peak of Cured-CNDs-7.5 attributed to the oxirane group in EPI weakened at 875 cm−1 [29], and the absorption peak of CNDs at 1710 cm−1 disappeared, but a new absorption peak appeared at 1730 cm−1. These results illustrate that the reaction between the carboxyl group of CNDs and the oxirane group in EPI weakens the signal of the oxirane group at 875 cm−1, and the formed ester bond will generate a new absorption peak of CO at 1730 cm−1 [32].

The covalent cross-linking network of CNDs with EPI can also be tested by classic swelling equilibrium experiments. In this work, all samples in toluene, dichloromethane, and THF show the properties of covalent cross-linking networks nature with swelling but no dissolution. Moreover, as increase the loading of CNDs, the sol gel fraction and swelling ratio decreases, which is consistent with the increase in crosslinking density of the equilibrium swelling experiment results (Fig. 1B.). The torque change of the crosslinking reaction kinetics of the sample tested at Rotorless rheometer 180 °C exhibits classic curing profiles by increasing the torque during the curing process (Fig. S8.) implying that the CNDs is capable of crosslinking EPI. The torque gradually reaches to a plateau over time, indicating that the crosslinking reaction is complete, the torque enlarged as the content of CNDs increases, which is consistent with the test results of crosslink density and modulus. Moreover, the non-linear change of torque with time also shows that DMI can promote the formation of cross-linked network [33].

The mechanical properties of CNDs samples are investigated as shown in Fig. 1C and D, and the detailed parameters are summarized in Table 1 . The vitrified rubber composite prepared by this interfacial crosslinking shows a significant reinforcement effect with the increase of CNDs loading. For example, the sample CNDs-2.5 exhibits very low mechanical strength (~2.1 MPa), but has a high elongation at break (~820%), while the CNDs-12.5 sample shows very high mechanical strength (~15 MPa), which is 6 times increment compare to the sample CNDs-2.5. The main reason could be attributed to the simultaneous increase of crosslink density and filler loading to CNDs-12.5, which results more shorter network chain breaking before large deformation and more nanofillers restricting the movement of the rubber chain [34]. And in order to clarify the cross-linking and strengthening characteristics of CNDs to EPI, vitrified rubber composite with the same carboxyl group as our CNDs-12.5 was prepared and cross-linked with sebacic acid (SA) as a comparison. To our surprise, the tensile strength of CND-12.5 is 2.1 times than that of the sample with the same carboxylic acid content cross-linked with SA. This remarkable reinforcement is may due to the following two aspects. Firstly, the dynamic chemical exchange interface between CNDs and EPI improves the dispersion of CNDs in the rubber matrix (Fig. S10.), which is a necessary condition for the efficient reinforcement of CNDs as nano-fillers. On the other hand, in addition to the dynamic chemical bond interaction between CNDs and EPI, some EPI molecules may also move to the surface of CNDs and being adsorbed, which is difficult to disengage again as free molecules (Scheme 1.), forming physical cross-linking points [35,36]. This also account the reason why SA and CNDs-12.5 have roughly the same content of carboxyl groups serve as cross-linking points, but show different swelling ratio in the equilibrium swelling test (Table 1.). The DMA test results are also consistent with the above analysis (Fig. 1D.). The storage modulus(Eʹ) gradually increases in the full temperature range while the peak of loss factor (tanδ) gradually decreases with the increase of CNDs content. Moreover, unlike uncrosslinked molecules, the Eʹ of all samples reach to a plateau after the glass transition temperature (Tg) instead of falling to zero, which implies that the network maintains structural integrity even at elevated temperatures, and further indicates that this network is a permanent dynamic cross-linked network, the connectivity of the cross-linked network remained [13,16,37,38].

The rubber composites with a distinctive dynamic exchange interface design provides a unique opportunity to create various 3D structures (Fig. 3A.) with great versatility, thanks to the dynamic nature of β-hydroxyl esters, which is capable of releasing the external stress applied to the networks through continuous dynamic exchange reaction above activation temperature of dynamic exchange bond. To demonstrate this strategy experimentally, for example, the strain value of the samples in the creep test at room temperature maintains a basically constant while at elevated temperature(180 °C), the strain increases linearly with time like a viscous liquid flow (Fig. 2 A and 2B.). This phenomenon demonstrated that the vitrified rubber composite prepared by this dynamic exchange interface strategy is similar to the permanent crosslinking characteristics of traditional vulcanized rubber at room temperature, but when the temperature rises above the dynamic exchange temperature, the continuous dynamic exchange reaction triggered in the network to relaxing external stress makes the strain raised linearly over time, similar to thermoplastics [16,38]. Moreover, judging from the stress relaxation performance of CNDs-7.5 at different temperatures, it can relax the network stress to less than 1/e of the original value through dynamic exchange reaction in a reasonable short time at all test temperatures, which further implies that we prepared vitrified rubber composite material should have good reprocessing properties. Significantly, CNDs-7.5 can completely relax externally applied stress through the dynamic exchange reaction of β-hydroxyl esters despite its covalently crosslinked nature. As the stress reaches 37% of the initial value (Fig. 2C.), the characteristic relaxation time τ* is recorded for CNDs-7.5 at different temperatures. By fitting the Arrhenius law, the activation energy of CNDs-7.5 is calculated to be 67 kJ/mol (Fig. 2D.), which is lower than the vitrimer fabricated based on the transesterification reaction reported by Leibler et al.(88 kJ/mol) [13]. The possible reason may due to the difference in crosslinking density. In those epoxy resin systems, the crosslinking density is very high, which require more energy input to trigger dynamic exchange rection in per volume. Although the activation energy increases slightly with the increase of CNDs content (Fig. S11.), the activation energy value is still lower than reported in the literature [13], which indicates that our system may have better reprocessing performance in the subsequent recycling process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reshape and reprocessability of the vitrified rubber composite
ce:section.ce:para: For the thermosetting polymer with permanent cross-linked network, it is impossible to relax the network stress through dynamic exchange reaction while maintaining the integrity of the network, thus the shape of which is permanently fixed once cured [12,39,40]. Compared with thermosetting materials, the vitrified materials can activate the exchange reaction of dynamic crosslinking bonds through thermal stimulation when subjected to external forces, so as to relax the network stress. If the external force totally relaxed, it means that a remodeling process has been completed. As shown in Fig. 3A, the vitrified rubber composite can be wrought from thin strips to a variety of 3D shapes (zigzag, heart-shaped, Spiral rolls et al.), which achieve reshaping through heat trigger dynamic exchange reaction.

Moreover, the reprocessability of the samples with different CNDs loading has been investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, vulcanized sample of CNDs-7.5 was cut into pieces and hot-pressed again, and the surface flatness of the reprocessed sample is similar to that of the fresh one. Besides, the tensile test results present that the reprocessed sample still maintains good mechanical performance, further indicating the excellent recyclability of them (Fig. 3C.). For the samples with low CNDs content, such as CNDs-2.5, the elongation at break and mechanical strength can be recovered by 96.3% and 96.2%, respectively (Fig. 3D and Fig. S13A.). Nonetheless for the samples with high CNDs content, the elongation at break recovered well, with relatively obvious decline in mechanic strength (Fig. 3D and Figs. S13C and D.). This difference may be attributed to the following two aspects. Firstly, with the increase of the loading of CNDs, the restriction of nano-fillers on the movement of molecular chains is also enhanced [35,36], which is not conducive to the rearrangement of the network. Secondly, a high content of CNDs means a high moles of carboxyl groups, but the relative amount of epoxy groups corresponding to each carboxyl group decreases, which means that it is difficult to achieve complete network rearrangement. In addition, FT-IR (Fig. S12.) shows that the structure of the samples after reprocessing have no significant change, which indicates that the damage to the rubber material after reprocessing is negligible.

Shape memory polymers (SMPs), is a new class of programmable stimuli-responsive shape-changing polymers, which are capable of fixing their temporary shape and restoring the permanent shape under external stimulus (heating, lighting, PH, etc) [41,42]. The vitrified rubber composite we prepared is a covalent cross-linked polymer with a glass transition temperature near 0 °C (Fig. 1D.), which has great potential as shape memory materials. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the rectangular spline can be folded into a right-angled shape and fixed into a temporary shape at −10 °C, which can be restored to its original shape upon heating. Due to the dynamic cross-linking network, the rectangular spline can further reshape the permanent shape to a spiral through the reconfiguration of the cross-linking network above the dynamic exchange temperature. This permanent spiral coil is then rolled up, placed at −10 °C to fix the shape, and finally reheated. Surprisingly, it quickly returns to the spiral roll shape again (see the movie I). In addition, the quantitative DMA test results (Fig. 4B) show that the vitrified rubber composite has high fixation ratio (96%) and recovery ratio (86%), which has potential as a smart material applied in various fields (sensors, biomedical devices, aerospace applications, etc) [43,44].

In summary, in order to achieve the balance of crosslinking, reinforcement and recycling, we present a green methodology with a solvent-free process to construct dynamic β-hydroxyl ester bonds at the interface between EPI and CNDs, in one step achieving cross-linking, reinforcement and reprocessability of vitrified polyisoprene composite. CNDs serves as both crosslinker and reinforcement center, and this dynamic covalent exchange interface design improves the dispersion of CNDs in the rubber matrix, which is a promising avenue for efficient nano-reinforcement. Moreover, the vitrified rubber composite showed a significant reinforcement with increasing the loading of CNDs, which is 2.1 times than that of the sample with the same carboxylic acid content cross-linked with SA. Besides, the vitrified rubber composite also exhibits excellent shape memory performance, which has potential as a smart material applied in various fields (sensors, biomedical devices, aerospace applications, etc). We believe that this green sample preparation protocol combined with dynamic covalent chemistry (DCC) strategies offered a greener and more sustainable avenue towards future development of rubber materials.
The manuscript was written by the first author. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 51873203), the Key Projects of Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan (no. 20200401041GX, no. 20200401042GX), Talent Development Grants Centralized Funding Project of Jilin Province (no. Y9390002), and the Special Fund Supported Project for High-tech Industrialization of Science and Technology Cooperation between Jilin Province and the Chinese Academy of Sciences (2019SYHZ0003).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2021.123864.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocomposites from styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) and multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) part 1: morphology and rheology

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymer free volume and its connection to the glass transition
